references.title: Explainable artificial intelligence-machine learning models to estimate overall scores in tertiary preparatory general science course

extracted.Features:
[Assignment 1 Mark, Assignment 2 Mark, Assignment 3 Mark,
Assignment 4 Mark, Assigment 5 mark, Exam mark]
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Authors perform a correlation analysis and identify Exam Grade to have a 0.72 correlation with the target variable.
extracted.Models:
SVR
ELM
XGBoost
MLP
ExtraTrees
GBoost
Deep Neural Networks
AdaBoost
Bagging
LightGBM
Random Forest
DT
extracted.Assessment Strategy: Holdout Method (64% train,  16% val and 20% test)
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: {RRMSE: 0.37% - SVR Model 6 - includes all predictors, MAE: 0.268 - SVR Model 6 - includes all predictors, RMAE: 0.33% - SVR Model 6 - includes all predictors}
extracted.Comments:
Authors create 6 SVR models (one after eaxh moment of evaluation).

Authors examine two modelling frameworks. The first is sequencial: M1 refers to Assignment 1 and all subsequent models have the addition of the next assignment.

In framework 2, the authors start with Exam grade and start adding each assignment grade).

Authors do feature interpretation using LIME and SHAP and find that Exam is, unsurprisingly, the most important predictor of the final grade.

Early prediction is possible as early as Week 2.
extracted.Limitations:
Any model past Model 6 cannot be used for eacly prediction since it uses all elements of evaluation that are factored in to compute the final grade.

Model optimization strategy and computyational cost of the techniques selected.

Needs additional predictors from other sources and generalization across institutions.

annotations.text: Authors use 5 assignments and one exam mark as predictors to overall scores of students. The novelty factors from their combination of Tree-Structured Parzen Estimation and SVR. Authors evaluate their approach against other models on a sample of 492 students taking a general science course. Models are also interpreted using LIME and SHapley.

Results show that their model outperforms all others used, achieving the lowest RMSE, RRMSE, highest Wilmott’s index, Legates-McGabe index (LM) and Nash-Sutcliffe Efficiency. LIME also shows that exam grade is the most important factor.
